data.title: Brent could jump to $120/bbl over the coming weeks – Citi
data.description:
Brent crude oil (CO1:COM) is expected to climb to $120 per barrel over the next few weeks as tensions between Iran and Israel continue to roil energy markets, according to Max Layton, Citi’s global head of commodities research.

The commodity is currently trading around $100, representing a significant potential jump as the conflict shows few signs of abating. The rally in oil prices has already delivered a substantial blow to the global economy.

In an interview with CNBC, Layton explained that the cost of crude oil and petroleum products has surged from approximately $2.5T at the start of the year to an annualized $4.5T—a shock equivalent to 2% of global GDP.

“The two sides are still very, very far apart,” Layton said, noting that while negotiations may be occurring, it remains difficult to see how demands from both Iran and Israel can be reconciled in the short term.

In a worst-case scenario, oil prices (CL1:COM), (CO1:COM) could spike dramatically higher if disruptions at the Strait of Hormuz persist.

Layton outlined a “bull case” where Brent (CO1:COM) reaches $150 per barrel, with total prices including product premiums climbing to $200 or more—levels not seen since the 2008 spike. If the conflict continues for two to three months, the economic damage will rival the combined impact of both 1970s oil shocks, resulting in a devastating 6% to 7% hit to global GDP.

The best-case scenario, however, would see a rapid de-escalation and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz. Under this outcome, Layton expects prices to fall back to the $70 to $80 range relatively quickly. He characterized a loss of 200 to 300 million barrels of production as “manageable” if a resolution comes within the week.

Citi’s baseline view remains that both sides will eventually step back from the brink after experiencing the severe economic and military costs of prolonged conflict.

“The baseline view is that seeing that more fully and starting to feel that over the next four weeks results in the resolution,” Layton said. By year’s end, he expects prices to return to approximately $80 per barrel, roughly in line with current forward pricing.

Layton noted that while current volume losses actually exceed those experienced during the 1970s oil crises, the critical variable is duration. The 1970s oil shocks lasted five to six months, whereas Citi anticipates this crisis will be resolved in four to six weeks as both sides fully comprehend the consequences of continued escalation.

Oil ETFs: (USO), (UCO), (DBO), (OILK), and (USL).

Natural Gas ETFs: (UNG), (BOIL), and (UNL).

data.title: $14T debt wave looms, threatening higher rates and wider spreads, Apollo warns
data.description:
A surge in high-quality debt issuance is expected to test investor demand and keep upward pressure on borrowing costs, according to Apollo’s chief economist Torsten Slok.

In total, roughly $14T in investment-grade supply could come to market over the next year, driven by a combination of public and private sector financing needs. 

A significant portion stems from the U.S. government, which must refinance approximately $10T in existing debt over the next 12 months while also funding an estimated $2T budget deficit. 

At the same time, corporate borrowers are poised to add to the supply wave, with gross investment-grade issuance projected to reach around $2T in 2026. Increased borrowing from large technology firms, particularly hyperscalers, is expected to play a key role. 

Taken together, the influx of new debt is likely to create a more competitive environment for capital, putting upward pressure on Treasury yields and widening credit spreads as investors demand higher compensation to absorb the growing supply. 

“The bottom line is that the growing supply of investment grade fixed income product is putting upward pressure on rates and credit spreads,” Slok said. 

U.S. Treasury Yields: (US2Y), (US5Y), (US10Y), (US20Y), and (US30Y).

Fixed Income ETFs: (TLT), (TLH), (IEF), (IEI), (SHY), (SGOV), (SCHO), (BIL), (AGG), (BND), (VCIT), (MUB), (MBB), (JNK), (LQD), (HYG), (VTIP), (TIP), (SCHP), (STIP), (TIPX), (SPIP), (WIP), (GTIP), (LQDI), and (RINF).

data.title: ImmunityBio falls as FDA issues warning over Anktiva ad, podcast
data.description:
ImmunityBio (IBRX) shares dropped ~21% in the morning hours on Tuesday after the U.S. FDA issued a warning letter regarding a TV advertisement and a podcast that it alleged spread false and misleading information related to the company’s bladder cancer drug Anktiva.

In the letter to ImmunityBio (IBRX) CEO Richard Adcock, the regulator said that its Office of Prescription Drug Promotion has determined that the TV commercial and podcast titled "Is the FDA BLOCKING Life Saving Cancer Treatments?” violated federal law.

The promotional materials misbrand Anktiva and make the distribution of the immunotherapy in violation of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act, the FDA said. The agency added that the TV ad and podcast also falsely suggest that the drug is approved for new indications for which the company has yet to receive U.S. approval.

Anktiva is available in the U.S. only with Bacillus Calmette-Guérin ((BCG)) therapy for adults with BCG-unresponsive non-muscle invasive bladder cancer ((NMIBC)) with carcinoma in situ, with or without papillary tumors.

The FDA required ImmunityBio (IBRX) to submit a written response to the letter within 15 working days and threatened to take legal action if the company failed to take sufficient measures to address the violations.

data.title: Zoox adds Austin and Miami to its robotaxi testing ground
data.description:
Zoox (ZOOX) announced on Tuesday that it is close to launching robotaxi service in Austin and Miami on a limited basis to employees and their families as a final step before opening up the service to the public. 

Currently, Zoox (ZOOX) is offering free driverless rides around the Las Vegas Strip and certain San Francisco neighborhoods while also testing in 8 other cities. As part of a measured rollout, Zoox (ZOOX) will have 100 robotaxis on public roads once its service scales in Las Vegas and San Francisco, and it begins testing in Austin and Miami.

Zoox (ZOOX) showcased its robotaxi in Austin earlier this month during the South by Southwest Conference and Festival. As part of the initial launch, Zoox (ZOOX) robotaxis will operate in popular ride-hailing neighborhoods, although the precise service area was not formally articulated.

Notably, Zoox (ZOOX) disclosed that its robotaxis have driven nearly two million autonomous miles and carried over 350K riders to date.

Zoox (ZOOX) stands out from Tesla (TSLA) and Waymo (WAYMO) because its robotaxi is more of an electric, bidirectional, driverless shuttle designed specifically for on-demand urban ride-hailing rather than private ownership. It has a compact, pod-like body with no front or rear, four-wheel steering, and roof-mounted sensor towers at each corner that provide overlapping 360-degree vision and long-range perception. Sliding doors on each side open to a flat, low floor and an interior with four carriage-style seats facing one another in a symmetric "living-room-like" cabin. 

 

 

 

As expected with a true robotaxi, there is no steering wheel or driver's seat; instead, each passenger gets an equivalent seat with their screen and controls for climate, media, and lighting. The autonomous system combines data from cameras, lidar, radar, and infrared to perceive its surroundings, predict what other road users will do, and plan a path for Level 4 driverless operation in mapped urban zones.

data.title: Markets could take some time to recover even with an Iran deal possibly coming soon – Julius Baer
data.description:
Mark Matthews, head of research for Asia at Julius Baer, dismissed President Trump’s Friday deadline for Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz as meaningless, noting that previous deadlines have already come and gone without consequence.

Despite the geopolitical uncertainty dragging down U.S. stocks (SP500), (DJI), (COMP:IND), Matthews expressed optimism that a diplomatic resolution could emerge soon.

“The deadline was supposed to be this morning, and it didn’t happen. So, I don’t think that Friday deadline means anything,” Matthews said in an interview with CNBC.

However, he cautioned investors against expecting an immediate market recovery, suggesting that equities face fundamental headwinds regardless of any Iran deal.

The strategist warned against what he called “magical thinking” about markets bouncing back quickly, pointing to technical factors pulling stocks lower. He noted that the S&P 500 (SP500) requires at least a 10% correction to satisfy technical analysts, while the index has only declined about 6% from its peak.

“I have a kind of conflicting message for you because I do think the prospect for a deal by early March is possible, but I also think that the stock market should go down more,” Matthews explained.

Matthews expressed particular concern about concentration in U.S. technology stocks (VGT), (XLK), (IYW), noting that eight of the ten largest companies globally are now American tech firms compared to just two a decade ago.

He highlighted that rising capital expenditures among hyperscalers have caused their “free cash flow to literally collapse as a percent of their income,” limiting their ability to fund buybacks and dividends.

Rather than moving to cash, Julius Baer is advising high-net-worth clients to rotate their portfolios toward other regions, particularly China and Hong Kong.

Matthews cited improved economic decision-making in China and a “slow and steady appreciation” of the renminbi as tailwinds, adding that Hong Kong landlords are trading at “discounts to their net asset value of over 50%” even as physical property prices rise.

Looking ahead, the bank forecasts that crude oil prices (CL1:COM) will stabilize in the high sixties within three months.

While acknowledging that Asia remains vulnerable to energy disruptions, Matthews maintained that the region offers “the best potential” for equity growth over the coming years once the current geopolitical crisis subsides.

data.title: Wall Street falls as Iran tensions weigh on market sentiment
data.description:
Wall Street’s major market averages pushed lower on Tuesday as traders continued to monitor developments related to the U.S.-Israel-Iran conflict.

The blue-chip Dow (DJI) was -0.8%, while the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was -0.8%, and the tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -1%. 

From a sector-by-sector stance, nine of the 11 S&P sectors are hovering in the red, with info tech suffering the most. At the other end, energy has been the strongest performer so far, with oil (CL1:COM) back on the move higher. 

U.S. Treasury yields continued to move higher. The shorter end 2-Year Treasury yield (US2Y) was up 5 basis points to 3.90%, and the popular 10-Year Treasury yield (US10Y) was up 6 basis points to 4.41%. 

Hopes of tensions easing in the Middle East came into the picture after President Donald Trump said the U.S. would postpone military action against Iran and the two countries were close to signing a deal. 

However, an Israeli official said Tuesday that any deal to end the war with Iran does not appear close, suggesting the purported diplomacy may be a tactic to buy time as Israel presses ahead with military operations in Iran and Lebanon, according to a media report.

On the economic calendar, U.S. PMI Composite data edged down in March, while manufacturing PMI rose.

As for stocks that were on the move, Corning Incorporated (GLW) climbed +3.6%, and shares of Estée Lauder Companies (EL) fell -8.1%.

data.title: It will take months for an agreement before oil prices return to $70-80 range – analyst
data.description:
Oil prices (CL1:COM), (CO1:COM) will take a couple of quarters to return to the comfortable $70-80 per barrel range even after a diplomatic agreement is reached on the Strait of Hormuz crisis, according to Aditya Saraswat, Rystad Energy’s Research Director for the MENA region.

In an interview with CNBC, Saraswat cautioned that despite recent headlines suggesting negotiations between the U.S. and Iran, the resumption of energy trade through the critical waterway remains months away.

The current market volatility is being driven by “headline arbitrage” rather than fundamentals, Saraswat explained, pointing to the whiplash that followed President Trump’s Truth Social post suggesting negotiations with Iran were underway.

Prices dropped nearly 10% before recovering when Iranian officials denied the talks were happening.

The Rystad analyst emphasized that this is “a military and a political problem, which is something that energy markets and especially the oil and gas markets cannot solve on its own.”

The disruption has already become the largest in the history of oil markets, with active field closures occurring across the region. The Strait of Hormuz accounts for approximately 20% of global oil and LNG trade, with the majority flowing toward Asian markets.

“We are months away from resumption of energy trade through the Strait of Hormuz,” Saraswat said, noting that even once de-escalation begins, there will be an additional lag of about a month before supply resumption can follow.

Operational challenges will further complicate any recovery. With storage facilities at full capacity, operators in the Gulf will need two to four weeks to bring shut-in fields back to pre-conflict production levels, depending on the maturity of the fields and any damages incurred during the shutdown process.

Even after traffic begins to normalize, high premiums will persist as the market tests the durability of any political resolution. Saraswat expects shipping traffic to start at reduced levels and “trickle back” to pre-conflict volumes gradually rather than rebounding immediately.

The analyst suggested that diplomatic breakthroughs could potentially emerge over the coming weeks but stressed that normalization of supply will happen gradually and remains contingent on achieving a durable political resolution between the parties involved.

Oil ETFs: (USO), (UCO), (DBO), (OILK), and (USL).

Natural Gas ETFs: (UNG), (BOIL), and (UNL).
